name: Adarsh Rai
learning_goal: First of all, I want to say that I am a huge  fan of Sandesh Sir and his mesmerising music. "Aaoge Jab Tum", " Sooraj Hua Maddham", " Sajna Ve Sajna", " Akelo Chal Padiyo" are some of my favourites from his numerous beautiful compositions. I want to learn from Sandesh Sir because his music reflects a rare balance of classical purity and contemporary sensibility. His compositions feel rooted in classical understanding while remaining contemporary and cinematic. This is exactly the space I aspire to explore as a songwriter. Learning directly from him is a rare opportunity which will help me to understand the techniques as well as the actual process behind creating soulful songs.
learned_music: Yes, I have learnt Hindustani classical music from Shilpayan, a music hub situated in Varanasi.
current_journey: My artiste journey so far has been rooted in words, raagas, and emotions. Throughout my journey, I have the privilege to learn Hindustani classical music, studying English literature, and working as a copywriter in an advertising agency. Words have been the centre of my life, and it's literally true if I say," I live in a world of words." What is still to be unfolded is bringing all these skills and experiences together to craft songs that breathe, live, thrive, and have a life of their own.

name: Yuvraj Kumar
learning_goal:
Sandesh Shandilya’s music has a purity and simplicity that really speaks to me. He blends Indian classical roots with a very honest, contemporary way of telling a story, and that’s the direction I want to grow in.
I’m not someone who was gifted or trained from childhood, I actually came to music only a few years ago after a moment in my life pushed me to drop everything and start learning. Whatever I know today is from curiosity and reading. In many ways, it feels like music found me.
That’s why learning from him matters to me, it’s not just about technique, it’s about understanding a musical philosophy I truly connect with.
current_journey: There is still a lot to unfold in my artiste journey, my signature sound, my own compositional language, and the ability to translate emotion into music with depth and clarity. I’ve discovered structure and technique, but the spiritual and emotional layers of my artistry are still blooming. What I’m seeking now is the intersection of discipline and intuition, where craft meets instinct and the music truly becomes my own.

name: Aayat
learning_goal:
I heard Sandesh Shandilya say in a podcast that ‘Aaoge Jab Tum O Saajna’ wasn’t composed, it just arrived… like a divine revelation. And for the first time, something a creator said matched exactly what I had felt for yearsThat song came to me when I was in 5th grade. I don’t even know how at that age a song became my escape, but it did. It stayed with me through pauses in life, through heartbreaks, and honestly through things that hurt more than heartbreak. It was just… there.I don’t have musical training. I don’t know the technicalities. I only know that I feel things deeply and I’ve never known how to channel all these vague, raw pieces of me. Music is the only place where my questions settle a little.
And the idea that the same song that once held all my pain could now guide me through its own creator feels like life coming full circle. That’s why I want to learn from him.
current_journey: Honestly, my artiste journey hasn’t even begun yet. Everything I feel and imagine is still vague… but there’s a part of me that keeps telling me I’m meant to learn how to channel this. I may not have technical knowledge or even create songs yet, but with the right guidance, I know I can make sense of all that I carry inside and grow into the artiste I’m supposed to become.
remarks: Not Interested

name: Aazad
learning_goal:
I want to deconstruct the 'silence' in his music. Sandesh Shandilya has this way of making a pause feel as powerful as a crescendo. For my own evolution, I’m looking to bridge the gap between the rhythmic urgency of hip-hop and the timeless, melodic soul of Bollywood.
It’s about more than just a beat; it’s about capturing that 'magic' where the music feels like a story being told, not just a track being played. Bringing that cinematic flavor to a versatile pop sound is where my next chapter begins.
current_journey:
For me, the journey is about breaking the boundaries of genre. I’ve built a solid foundation in hip-hop, but now I’m looking to infuse that grit with pop sensibilities to create something more versatile.
It’s not just about changing the sound; it’s about exploring how a catchy hook can live alongside a complex verse. I want my music to feel as intentional as a curated gallery piece—where the rhythm hits hard, but the melody sticks with you long after the track ends. Being 'versatile' isn't just a goal; it's the new standard for my sound.
